svg.title: Dangerzone Rust OCR module flow
svg.desc: Architecture and call-order diagram for the Rust OCR implementation, showing lib.rs, ocr modules, kreuzberg-tesseract wrappers, raw Tesseract bindings, and PDF rendering.
svg.text: OCR Module Structure and Internal Call Order
Reviewer-focused Rust flow: page pixels -> backend trait -> OCR words -> invisible PDF text layer
backend trait and OCR page model
not part of internal call order
This is the static Rust design behind the call flow above: shared OCR data types and a generic backend trait live in src/ocr/mod.rs.
marks methods introduced by this OCR PR. Dashed arrows show dependency boundaries or planned backend options; solid numbered arrows show Rust call order.
These notes explain what each numbered step does in reviewer-friendly language and point to the relevant source file.

g.text: It renders the input into page pixels, then chooses
integrated OCR or the plain/platform fallback path.
Runs the container-based renderer and collects stdout.
The result is not PDF yet; it is a byte stream with
page dimensions and raw RGB pixels for every page.
Turns the byte stream into structured PageData values.
Each page gets width, height, and pixels so later code
does not need to parse binary offsets again.
Concrete Linux OCR backend used for integrated OCR.
It is backed by the kreuzberg-tesseract dependency and
is called through the generic OcrBackend trait.
Default trait method that loops over rendered pages.
For each PageData it calls the backend's single-page
OCR method and collects OcrPage values.
It adapts raw RGB pixels for Tesseract, runs recognition,
and returns an OcrPage for that source page.
Finds the trained language data Tesseract needs.
It checks an explicit environment variable first, then
Normalizes paths for Tesseract data lookup.
If the path is already a tessdata directory, it is used;
otherwise the method appends a tessdata component.
This is where the backend uses the external OCR crate.
The diagram groups these calls so the reviewer can focus
Converts Tesseract's result iterator into Rust words.
Raw native iterator handling stays contained inside the

svg.text: The second half follows the extracted OCR words into the page model and then into the final PDF text layer.

g.text: Walks Tesseract's recognized words one by one.
The loop records line_id, skips empty or malformed results,
and stops when Tesseract has no next word.
Copies recognized UTF-8 text into a Rust String.
After copying, it calls the matching native delete
function so the C allocation is released.
Reads the word rectangle from Tesseract.
It translates left, top, right, bottom coordinates into
the local OcrVBox shape: x, y, width, and height.
The backend returns one Rust value per recognized word.
Each OcrWord carries text, a pixel-space box, and the
line_id used later to group words into text lines.
Wraps all words from one source page into one OCR result.
OcrPage owns the words and also provides text_lines()
Private helper used after integrated OCR completes.
It keeps the rendered pages and passes matching OCR pages
into the shared PDF file writer.
Shared file-writing helper for OCR and non-OCR output.
It checks that pages exist, opens the destination file,
and delegates PDF construction to write_pdf().
Builds the PDF structure: catalog, pages, images,
compressed pixel streams, content streams, and the
Adds invisible text commands to the page content stream.
It converts OCR pixels into PDF points, groups words with
OcrPage::text_lines(), and writes searchable hidden text.
The resulting file is still a safe raster PDF visually.
It also contains an invisible text layer so users can
